speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five of an interview with Rose Winterfeldt. You were saying that you were in the courtyard with Russian machine guns pointed at you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, at everybody in the-- from the cellar was pushed out into the courtyard. They put up the machine guns. And I remember saying [GERMAN] my beautiful young life will end here. But it didn't. They didn't shoot anybody. Instead, they herded us out of the building on to the street. Now, you have to understand, everything was burning. There were tanks. There were wounded soldiers all over the place. It was a real war scene. And we had to march through all of this. They wanted us-- they wanted us out of the inner city. They wanted to push us out toward east out of the city. OK, so we started marching through these burning streets. And it was in the middle of the night. And-- and at at one point, all of a sudden, the Russians took all the men away, including my father. We were separated from my father. Now, I-- my mother was a sickly woman. And she-- she wasn't even able to carry anything. And I had to support her. I didn't want her to collapse on me, you know, with my father gone. And we marched, and we marched, and we marched, day and night, until we reached an easterly outskirt of Berlin. And there were other people of the-- of the house we had lived in. And they said, you can stay with my sister or whatever. We spent one night there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother said, well, we have to find daddy. We have to find him. We can't live without him. And I had the-- I had an-- an idea to-- to-- to look up somebody. It is too long, the story. Anyway, after two and a half days, we were reunited with my father. The Russians had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] herded the men outside of the city, and then all of a sudden, said to them, now you can go home again, you know? Which was lucky. But my father was among all these Germans, you know? He had to suffer along with the Germans. That feeling is horrible. Well, we were united again. And then we settled in-- in one of the outskirts of Berlin or-- at which my father had left a suitcase while he was marched by the Russians on the-- on the-- on the highway or whatever you want to call that. And-- and he met a woman there and said, take my suitcase. I can't carry it anymore. And he-- she gave him the address. And he said, that's where we are going, where the suitcase is. We had no other place to go, you know? And that's where we ended up. And you remained there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We remained there from-- from May '45 until May '49. But of course, we started working in the city. I worked in West Berlin then already. My father too, you know? Well, we commuted again. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then '49, you were able to move into West Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Into West Berlin because I gave-- I-- I actually gave my parents and-- and my father an ultimatum. He was a man who-- who didn't like change. And I was 22 at the time. I worked in West Berlin and lived in the Russian zone. Where we lived was the Russian zone-- not the Russian sector of Berlin, but the zone, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to him, if we are not moving to West Berlin, I'm gonna to move alone, because a colleague of mine had said, you can move with me into my apartment, no problem. I'll give you a room. And then my father got scared that his daughter wants to move out, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we applied for-- with the American-- the Americans were in charge of the sector we would want to move in. And we applied with the American authorities for permission to move into West Berlin, and we got it within a week. And we moved. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where were you working?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I started working already in 1947 for the Jewish Community in Berlin. I had no formal education or anything. I was just a young girl. And I went in and said, here I am. Can you use me? And they said, yes, we need a switchboard girl. And I started as a switchboard girl, working there. It didn't last long. They put me into all other offices-- personnel office, payroll office. I made the rounds. And in the end, I ended up as being the secretary to the president of the Jewish Community until I got married in '56.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Now, your father also wanted to help the Jewish community.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: He-- he, after the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was a cantor, a teacher.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let me ask you about this Jewish community. How many Jews came back to Berlin? Who was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were 2,000 mixed marriages. 2,000 had lived underground in and around Berlin. And about 2,000 came from the camps to Berlin. So the community started with about 6,000 Jews. And there was one center where we were all registered and where help came from, and where slowly, everything-- the services started again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rosh Hashanah 1945 is also a day what will be with me for all my life, you know? I mentioned yesterday to you [? Rabbi ?] Martin Riesenburger. He and my father performed the first High Holiday services. That was something to-- to witness. Yeah. Where did they perform them? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: In a-- in a synagogue that was not destroyed-- not by bombs, not by the Nazis. It was a small synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's remarkable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And this Martin Riesenburger had saved Sefer Torahs. Rice. So we had Sefer Torahs. And we had everything we needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you reestablish a relationship with your husband-to-be after the war? And I know you ended up getting married in Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And as I mentioned before, on May 13 was the first time we were able to walk into the inner city to see what Anita is doing. But also, we wanted to go to our bombed-out house, or destroyed house. And when we got there, there was a sign-- Walter Winterfeldt and the address. And Walter Winterfeldt was the father of my boyfriend, Hans Winterfeldt. He was back from Auschwitz. And right away, we-- we-- we walked there where he was. And-- and he-- he had survived Auschwitz, of course, waiting for his wife and his son. And at that point, I went-- every week, I went to visit him. I walked. I mean, I had to walk 30 miles, but I walked every week to visit him and see any news, any news. And in-- in the end of June, I came. And then there was Kitty Winterfeldt, my boyfriend's mother. She came back. She had survived Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the wait began. July and August, and no word of Hans. No word of Hans. And then came the High Holidays, 1945. And it was also a week I visited them, before the High Holidays And my future mother-in-law said to me, oh, come to-- come into the bedroom. I want to show you something. And I thought she had gotten a dress for the High Holidays, because we all had nothing, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the bedroom, in the bed, was my future husband. He had come back. He had survived. And he was in the-- in a hospital for a long time. He must have been-- he doesn't-- I think he does not really know when he was liberated. But he survived, and he was there. And he recuperated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he went to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went-- in '47, he left, yes. He went to the United States. And there were-- distant cousins of his mother were living here. They helped him. He came here. He-- he-- he right away had a job here in the St. Regis Hotel in New York, in the kitchen. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then, in 1950, he was drafted. And he got the-- the GI Bill. And he went to City College and so on and so forth. But this is a story he should tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But how did the-- the marriage work out, that he was here in the United States and you were in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came-- yeah. In 1955-- I mean, we were in contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in contact.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in contact. And in 1955, he came over to Germany to visit, but also to push his restitution and his parents' restitution, you know? And I-- I was able to help him with that a little bit. And, well, we discovered we like and love each other. And next-- the next year, he came back and we got married. And also his parents, who also had immigrated to the United States, also came. So we were all together for our wedding in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you moved to the United States with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I moved, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents remained in Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned restitution.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the German government helping the Jews who were-- who were there in Berlin after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in every possible way they could. They were-- they-- right-- you mean right after the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the Germans didn't have anything to help anybody with. No. The-- then the American joined--

speaker: interviewer
text: The American joint--

speaker: interviewee
text: --joint helped us, and other Jewish organizations from outside Germany helped us. The Germans couldn't. The restitution laws came into being in 1951, you know? And these were laws, when they had to pay back-- really pay back millions, and millions, and millions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was also for Jews who were now living in Germany--

speaker: interviewee
text: Living--

speaker: interviewer
text: --as well as in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: For-- for everybody who survived, for everybody who had lost anything or anybody, they were all entitled to restitution.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now you-- you-- you worked in restitution here in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with a lawyer here, yes. He has-- had a restitution-- it was my first job when I came over. By-- by coincidence, I found that job. And he was very happy to have me. And I was happy to be there, and made good money, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked until you had your children--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- yeah. Until my son was born, yeah, I-- I worked-- not during the whole pregnancy. I stopped before. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I didn't work at all for 18 years. And I started working again in 1980, not knowing, really, what to do, what I could do, you know? I went to an-- a bilingual agency because I thought maybe I can use my German somehow, somewhere, in some trade business. I had no idea. And I went to that agency. It was a good agency on Park Avenue. And they said, well, we have a job for you. You want to go for the interview? I went to the interview, to a big law firm. I had no idea of law firms in that sense, these big law firms. They interviewed me and they hired me. It all happened within two hours. And I was there for 13 years. Now, we mentioned that you had returned to Berlin in 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But also, you all along had family there. So you must have--

speaker: interviewer
text: --been going there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every year. Every year, we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Every year.

speaker: interviewer
text: --perspective as a Jew, but also as someone who's been through Berlin quite a bit since the war, as to how the war is being handled there? What's-- what's the-- do people talk about it? Do--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the young generation--

speaker: interviewer
text: --people deal with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The young generation talks about it. But the old ones, no. People my age and older, they don't want to know about it. Of course not, because they feel so guilty or they were in it, you know? But the young generation-- I have great respect for the young German generation and for the good men who are now in their 50's who are really bringing this all out, you know? They were-- they really-- they all feel guilty for what their fathers and grandfathers did, in my opinion. And there is so much done as far as museums and-- and places of remembrance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speaking of what they did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how did the people in Berlin find out about what they did after the war? In other words--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --they didn't know during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: You must've found out from people-- Jews who returned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of-- of course. Of course they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what about the rest of the Berliners? Did they find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rest of the Germans? They probably would have said and probably said, well, we suffered too. But they forgot that they all were very much for Hitler, and-- and-- and that their suffering was a consequence that they were-- that they had brought onto themselves, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was their post-war attitude--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --we suffered as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: We suffered as well, sure. Sure. They didn't want to hear about it. And I-- I-- I must say, I didn't have-- I hardly had any contact with Germans. I worked for the Jewish Community. I lived within-- within our-- our-- our group. I-- I-- I couldn't. I wouldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you still had family that wasn't Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my family, that's a different story. But the-- the-- the people I knew who were good to us, that was fine. But people I didn't know, I was suspicious. And when I came to America and I rode for-- the subway here for the first time, I looked around me-- I remember that very vividly-- looked around and I said, none of these people were participating in the killing of my people. And I felt great. I felt free. No more suspicion. The suspicion of everybody is not healthy, you know? Not good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was glad to be-- but of course, I didn't leave-- or my parents didn't leave Germany because of my mother's condition. She was very ill, as you mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had crippling arthritis. It started very early in her age, in the early '30s. And of course, we couldn't do anything for her during that horrible time. And then after the war, with all the cold, and the hunger, and-- and-- and the worry, and the hardship, it got worse and worse and worse. And by 1947, she was completely paralyzed. Stiff. And in the end, in-- in-- in-- for 20 years of-- at least 18 years of her life, she just could move her eyes. That's it. Nothing else. Not her arms, not her head, not her hands, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think that they would have wanted to leave Germany if this had not been the case?

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in 1946, we-- we already had to applied to go to America, yes. I-- I remember that very much. But the Americans wouldn't let us come. We had a very good affidavit from distant relatives. And since being displaced persons, we would have gotten here easily. But we lived in the Russian zone. And people who lived in the Russian sector of Berlin, or in the Russian zone, the Americans wouldn't let in, fearful that we are communists, never mind what else you were. So that was impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about after you had moved to West Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, we were established. At that time, the Jewish community was blooming. My father had his-- the-- his place in life. He loved teaching. And he not only taught children, but he taught young men to be-- become cantors, you know? And he had his place. And they had a good living. They had a nice apartment. They had then restitution money. They-- they had no financial worries. They could do anything they wanted to. And no, there was no way to leave Germany at that time anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you had mentioned to me yesterday that at certain points when you were young, you were very religious and attended Orthodox services.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find that your experiences in Germany during the '40s, early '40s have-- affected your religious beliefs?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean my religious beliefs now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Changed your-- yes, now, and looking back.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that came much later. That came much later. At that time, I was still very, very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- at the end of the war and after the war, of course, very much so. And the older I got, and especially, I must say, after my son was born-- and I held this baby in my arms. And I thought of my little cousins, those babies, those little girls, and something happened to me. Something happened to me. I lost-- I lost faith, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's been 50 years or more-- a little more, is there any message that you would like to convey? Your thoughts?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In what respect? About your experience, looking back from this perspective.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this-- these horrible experiences of course shaped all our lives, shaped our characters, shaped the way we are. That a person like I am never had a real childhood or a happy youth-- that, of course, affected me. I was always a very serious girl and a very serious person. And the sadness stays with me until the day I die. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's still unbelievable what happened to our people. And I still have a hard time dealing with it. I try not to talk about it, but I felt this interview I had to do for everybody who comes after me and who wants to know what is possible, what people can do to people. It should be a warning. It should be a lesson. If anybody tramples on your rights, you should speak up and not let it go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And to everybody in America, I say be grateful to live in America, the greatest country, with all its freedoms and its rights. You have a right. You are protected. And I'm grateful to be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you wanted to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I wouldn't know what to add to that. You certainly told us a great deal, and we appreciate it. And we thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, was glad to do it. It was time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a unique perspective, having been in Berlin--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --through the entire war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband always said to me that I have to tell my story too because it's-- it's also unique. It is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Definitely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I did, and I'm glad I did. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're glad you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thank you. And I think the foundation for making it possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell us about these photos.

speaker: interviewee
text: These photos were saved by my father. He carried them with him all the time through everything. And this picture is a picture of my great-grandfather, Abraham Wolffenstein. He died in 1865 in Schloppe in West-Preussen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was taken at about 1864?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, right. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my great-grandmother, Fanny Wolffenstein, born [? Abramsson. ?] She was born in 1832 and died in 1912. She died in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was taken in the-- the 1860s?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the 1860s, yes, at the same time as her husband's picture was taken. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These are my grandparents, Max Lehmann and Jenny Lehmann, nee Wolffenstein. The picture was taken 1895. It was their engagement picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my father, Edmund Lehmann. The picture probably was taken in 1898 or '99.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Berlin also?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my father and his brothers. I start from the right, which is my father, Edmund Lehmann; then comes his brother Paul Lehmann, and the youngest is Alfred Lehmann. And the picture was taken about 1910 in Berlin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These are my mother's parents, Max and Emilie Kramer. And the picture was taken 1900 in Berlin. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is my mother, Gertrude Lehmann. And the picture was taken 1903 in Berlin. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This picture was taken 1927. The baby is myself, and my mom. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This picture, from left to right, is my grandma, Jenny Lehmann; then comes my father; then comes Hilde Kramer, my mother's sister; then comes my mother; then comes Paul Lehmann, Alfred Lehmann. And the little one sitting there is myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in 1929. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture is my grandma, Jenny Lehmann. On her lap is Lucy, my little cousin. And I'm standing in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think in spring of '35. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] All right. This picture was taken during a-- a rehearsal in the Jewish Kulturbund and for the performance of the which had a children's scene. In the very front row, the little girl in the middle is my cousin Lucy again. And then the girl to the left of her, with a dark collar on her little blouse, that is myself. And behind me, the two children are Ruth and Georg [? Pizarek, ?] whose father took the picture. And after the war, I was able to get a negative of this picture from him and have it developed. I do not know the name of the pianist. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my uncle Paul with Anita, his little daughter. And the picture is taken in 1940 in Berlin. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a class picture in-- taken on the first day of Hanukkah in 1941. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As you can see, we are all wearing our stars. And in the very last row, against the wall, the third head from the left, that is myself. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is my father's identification card from 1939. At that point, cards were issued with a J. And also, we had to sign our names with Israel for the man and Sara for the women. As you can see, it says Edmund Israel Lehmann. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] A picture of my father, taken approximately 1944. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The picture of my parents, taken in late '45. Myself, 1950. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture of my father is taken in 1965 in the synagogue in Berlin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my parents, taking-- taken in 1966 on the occasion of my father's 70th birthday. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my husband and myself and our children, Walter and Jane. And the picture was taken in 1967 in front of the house in Berlin where my parents lived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is 1987, with my two children-- Here?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Walter and Jane, here in Yonkers.
